JavaScript Core Concepts (19): Logical Operators and Faking Default Values Before ES6
JavaScript
JavaScript Core

Time to talk about logical operators. The thing about && and || in JavaScript is that they don't just return true or false — they return one of the actual operands, and if you don't watch out, the result can surprise you. We'll walk through &&, ||, and !, then use the OR trick to fake a function default value the old-school way, back before ES6 gave us real defaults.
/en/javascript/2020/06/28/javascript-core-15-operators-types-grammar-operators/
JavaScript Core Concepts (15): Operators Are Basically Tiny Functions
Operators, Types and Grammar

Next up: operators. You use them constantly no matter what you're building. This post covers what an operator actually is, why every operator is really just a tiny function under the hood, the operand counts (unary, binary, ternary), and what infix, prefix, and postfix expressions mean.
/en/javascript/2020/06/21/javascript-core-14-primitive-and-object-types/
JavaScript Core Concepts (14): Primitive Types vs. Object Types

The clearer you are on how types work in JavaScript, the easier real-world development gets. This post walks through the seven primitive types, the wrapper objects behind them, the famous typeof null bug, and why you should avoid declaring primitives with wrapper objects like new String().
/en/javascript/2020/06/21/javascript-core-13-dynamic-typing/
JavaScript Core Concepts (13): Dynamic Typing — Why You Never Declare Types

When you write JavaScript, you never have to declare what type a variable is. The reason is simple — JavaScript is a dynamically typed language. In this post I'll walk through what dynamic typing actually means, how it differs from a language like C, and the two type-conversion traps (explicit and implicit) that love to bite you.
/en/javascript/2020/06/13/javascript-core-12-asi-automatic-semicolon-insertion/
JavaScript Core (12): How Can Semicolons Be Optional? Meet ASI

How come JavaScript sometimes needs semicolons and sometimes doesn't? That's down to a mechanism called ASI — Automatic Semicolon Insertion. It's handy, but it can also bite you in surprising ways. This post walks through what ASI is, where it silently breaks your code, and the rules you should keep in mind.
/en/javascript/2020/06/07/javascript-core-11-statements-and-expressions/
JavaScript Core Concepts (11): Statements vs Expressions — The One Everybody Mixes Up

Statements vs expressions is one of those concepts almost everyone gets fuzzy on — me included. In this post we untangle the difference: why a variable declaration counts as a statement even though it has an equals sign, why expressions always return a value, and how function declarations, function expressions, blocks and object literals fit into the picture.
